summary:
Myasthenia gravis involves impairment at the neuromuscular junction leading to muscle weakness and fatigue; this condition primarily disrupts communication between nerves and muscles without affecting the peripheral nervous system directly. The disease mechanism centers around autoantibodies interfering with acetylcholine receptors, resulting in reduced neurotransmission efficiency. To summarize concisely:

Myasthenia gravis causes muscle weakness due to impaired neuromuscular transmission through antibodies targeting acetylcholine receptors, specifically disrupting signal transfer from motor neurons to skeletal muscles. This results in fluctuating symptoms that worsen with activity and improve after rest or cholinesterase inhibition therapy.
